1: 'Oh that my head were a spring of water and my eyes a fountain of tears, so that I could weep day and night for the slain of my dear people!' — the opening wish for supernatural capacity for grief establishes chapter 9 as the fullest expression of Jeremiah's prophetic sorrow. 'My dear people' (bat-ʿammî, daughter of my people) is an affectionate collective term for the community. The 'slain' (ḥalalê) are those killed in battle and by the siege — not yet historical events at the time of writing but prophetically certain. Jeremiah's tears are the human emotional counterpart to the divine grief that drives the judgment.
2: 'Oh that I had a lodging place for travelers in the wilderness, so that I could leave my people and go away from them!' — the wish for escape from his own people is the darkest expression of prophetic isolation. The contrast between Jeremiah 9:1-2 is striking: in Jeremiah 9:1 Jeremiah wishes he could weep more for them; in Jeremiah 9:2 he wishes he could flee from them. Both impulses are authentic: deep grief and deep revulsion coexist in the prophet's experience of a people he loves but who are 'all adulterers' (kî kullām mĕʾănāpîm) and 'a gathering of traitors' (ʿăṣeret bōgĕdîm). The tension between prophetic compassion and prophetic honesty is never resolved in Jeremiah.
3: 'They have bent their tongue like a bow to shoot falsehood; they have grown powerful in the land but not by truth' — the tongue-as-bow metaphor pictures deliberate deception as an aggressive act: lies are arrows aimed at specific targets. The 'growing powerful not by truth' (lōʾ lĕʾĕmûnāh gāberû bāʾāreṣ) describes the social-political success that the faithless have achieved — their prosperity proves not that their behavior is acceptable but that the covenant consequences have been temporarily deferred. 'They do not know me, declares the LORD' — yādaʿ in the covenant-intimacy sense: their practical behavior demonstrates the absence of genuine relationship, regardless of their religious vocabulary.
4: 'Beware of your neighbors; put no trust in any relative — for every brother is a schemer, and every neighbor goes around spreading slander' — the 'every brother is a Jacob' idiom: kol-ʾāḥ ʿāqôb yĕʿāqēb (every brother is a deceiver/Jacob). The verb ʿāqab means to supplant/trip up from behind; it is the root of the name Jacob (yaʿăqōb), who supplanted Esau (Genesis 25:26; Jeremiah 27:36: 'Is he not rightly named Jacob? He has supplanted me twice!'). Jeremiah's society has generalized the Jacob-trait: the competitive deception that characterized the patriarch's early life has become the universal social norm. The betrayal of family and neighbor destroyed the social trust that covenant community required.
5: 'Everyone deceives his neighbor, and no one tells the truth. They have trained their tongue to lie; they exhaust themselves committing sin' — limmĕdû lĕšônām dabbēr-šeqer (they have trained their tongue to speak falsehood) treats deception as an acquired skill: the ability to lie fluently requires practice and habituation. The exhaustion through sin (nileʾû ʿăwōtām) is the moral version of the physical exhaustion of pointless labor — sustained transgression that produces no genuine benefit and drains the transgressor. The spiritual-agricultural metaphor runs throughout the book: covenant violation is literally fruitless and tiring.
6: 'You live in the midst of deception; through their deceit they refuse to know me, declares the LORD' — the deception has become the social environment: there is no social space outside of it. The refusal to know YHWH is described as an active choice enabled by the deception — the lies people tell create an alternative reality that crowds out the true knowledge of YHWH. In the Johannine register, this is the dynamic of darkness that 'hates the light' (John 3:20); in Jeremiah's register, it is the social construction of a lie-world that makes covenant truth inaccessible.
7: 'Therefore this is what the LORD of hosts says: I will refine them and test them — for what else can I do with my sinful people?' — the divine query 'what else can I do?' (mâ-ʾeʿĕśeh) is one of the most remarkable expressions of divine perplexity in the OT. It does not indicate divine helplessness but divine faithfulness to a people who have exhausted all normal means of covenant restoration: warnings have been given, prophets have been sent, judgment has been threatened. The refining-and-testing metaphor (Proverbs 17:3; Jeremiah 27:21; Isaiah 48:10; Malachi 3:3) describes the purifying judgment: the exile will burn away the dross, but the process is painful for the metal.
8: 'Their tongue is a lethal arrow that shoots out deceit. With their mouths they speak peace to their neighbors, but in their hearts they are planning an ambush' — the peace-from-the-mouth / ambush-in-the-heart contrast is the prophetic diagnosis of covenant social collapse: the language of covenant solidarity (šālôm) is maintained while the practice of betrayal continues. This is what makes Jeremiah's era particularly dangerous: it is not openly hostile society but covertly treacherous society. The false peace-speaking is worse than open enmity because it prevents the betrayed party from protecting themselves.
9: 'Should I not punish them for all this? declares the LORD. Should I not take vengeance on such a nation as this?' — the double rhetorical question expects 'yes' and functions as a legal declaration of just cause for judgment. The identical formulation appears at 5:9 and Jeremiah 5:29, creating a refrain that punctuates the judgment oracles with legal self-justification. YHWH is not acting arbitrarily or out of wounded pride but in response to specific, documented covenant violations — the judgment is proportionate and just.
10: 'I will take up weeping and wailing for the mountains and lamentation for the wilderness pastures, for they have been laid waste so that no one passes through' — the mourning over the mountains and pastures personifies the land as a victim of the judgment, not merely its location. In the ANE, the land was understood as a moral agent responsive to the behavior of its inhabitants (Leviticus 18:25,28: the land vomits out its sinful inhabitants; Deuteronomy 11:17: the land refuses to yield its produce when YHWH withholds rain). The silence of the vacated land — no cattle, no birds, no wildlife — is the ecological consequence of the covenantal devastation.
11: 'I will make Jerusalem a heap of rubble, a haunt of jackals; I will make the cities of Judah a wasteland, with no one living in them' — the jackal-haunt (mĕʿôn tannîm) is one of the standard prophetic images for desolated cities (Isaiah 13:22; Jeremiah 34:13; Jeremiah 35:7; Jeremiah 10:22; Jeremiah 49:33; Jeremiah 51:37; Malachi 1:3). Jackals are scavengers that inhabit ruins — their presence marks a human settlement as definitively abandoned. The image was fulfilled in the post-586 BCE devastation of Judah, which archaeological surveys confirm as a dramatic population collapse across multiple sites. The Lachish Letters, written just before the Babylonian final assault, reflect a Judah rapidly losing its cities.
12: 'Who is wise enough to understand this? Who has received the word from the LORD and can explain it? Why has the land been ruined and laid waste like a desert that no one crosses?' — the wisdom-challenge is addressed to the sage class (Jeremiah 8:8-9): the wisest interpreter of events is the one who can explain why the land has been destroyed. The question challenges the covenant-logic skeptics who attributed Judah's difficulties to political misfortune or divine indifference rather than to covenant violation. The answer in Jeremiah 9:13-14 is programmatic: abandonment of the Torah + Baal-following = destruction.
13: 'Because they have abandoned my law that I placed before them, and have not obeyed my voice or followed it' — the two-part answer: law abandonment + voice disobedience. 'My law that I placed before them' (tôrātî ʾăšer nātattî lipnêhem) refers specifically to the written Torah — the covenant document that Moses deposited beside the ark (Deuteronomy 31:26) and that Josiah's reform had rediscovered (2 Kings 22:8). The written law's public availability makes its abandonment inexcusable: the people had access to the covenant terms and chose to disregard them.
14: 'Instead they followed the stubbornness of their own hearts and went after the Baals, just as their ancestors had taught them' — šĕrîrût libbām (the stubbornness of their hearts) appears again as Jeremiah's signature diagnosis. The generational transmission of Baal worship — 'just as their ancestors had taught them' — points to the family-based religious formation described in Jeremiah 7:18 (children, fathers, and women all participating in the queen of heaven cult). The apostasy is not individual but systemic: it has been embedded in the intergenerational transmission that Torah commanded for covenant faithfulness (Deuteronomy 6:7) but that has been redirected to Baal instead.
15: 'I will feed this people with wormwood and give them poisoned water to drink' — wormwood (laʾănāh) was an extremely bitter plant (Artemisia absinthium, still used in absinthe); to eat it was proverbially associated with suffering and injustice (Proverbs 5:4; Amos 5:7; Jeremiah 6:12). Poisoned water (mê rōʾš) continues the bitter-water image from Jeremiah 8:14. In contrast to the 'spring of living water' that YHWH is (Jeremiah 2:13), the covenant-cursed people receive its opposite — bitter, poisonous sustenance. Revelation 8:11 (the star Wormwood poisoning the waters) draws on this prophetic tradition.
16: 'I will scatter them among nations that neither they nor their ancestors have known; and I will pursue them with the sword until I have consumed them' — the scattering among unknown nations intensifies the covenant-curse of Deuteronomy 28:36,64 ('the LORD will drive you and the king you set over you to a nation unknown to you or your fathers'). The 'unknown nations' are specifically non-Israel's covenant context: no temple, no land, no ancestral heritage, no community of Torah-observance. The sword-pursuit until consumption describes the sustained campaign: the exile will not be a brief diplomatic deportation but a comprehensive destruction.
17: 'Consider this carefully; summon the professional mourning women to come; send for the most skilled among them to come' — professional mourners (hamĕqônenôt, the wailing women; hahăkāmôt, the skilled ones) were an institutionalized profession in the ancient Near East. In Israel, Egypt, and Mesopotamia, trained women were employed to amplify the communal grief at funerals through rhythmic wailing, composing and performing dirges (qînôt). Their professional skill was the ability to generate and sustain grief rituals that helped communities process collective loss. Jeremiah summons them because the coming loss will be too great for amateur lamentation.
18: 'Let them come quickly and raise a lament over us, so that our eyes may stream with tears and our eyelids overflow with weeping' — the description of extreme grief — tears streaming from the eyes and overflowing from the eyelids — is the physiological language of Lamentations 1:16 ('my eye, my eye overflows with water'). The weeping is communal ('over us') — the professional mourners lead the community's grief rather than performing it on behalf of passive bystanders. In the ANE mourning context, the external wailing of the professionals was understood to draw out and legitimize the internal grief of the bereaved community.
19: 'For a cry of wailing is heard from Zion: We are devastated! We are utterly disgraced — because we have had to leave the land, because our homes have been torn down' — the Zion lament anticipated in prophetic vision. The two causes of shame given — leaving the land (kî ʿāzabnû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ) and destroyed homes (kî hišlîkû miškĕnôtênû) — capture the two dimensions of the exile disaster: territorial displacement and household destruction. The land was the gift of the covenant; the home was the social unit of covenant practice. Both were lost simultaneously in the Babylonian deportation.
20: 'Now, women, hear the word of the LORD; receive the word from his mouth. Teach your daughters to mourn; teach one another this dirge' — the address to women connects the professional mourning of Jeremiah 9:17-18 to the generational transmission of communal grief practices. Just as the queen of heaven worship was transmitted from mothers to daughters (Jeremiah 7:18), the mourning for the covenant's collapse must also be transmitted in the female sphere. The dirge to be taught is not merely a cultural grief expression but a covenant testimony: this is what happens when YHWH's people abandon the covenant.
21: 'For death has climbed through our windows and entered our palaces, cutting down children in the streets and young men in the public squares' — the personification of death as an invader who enters through windows is found in Canaanite-Ugaritic mythology, where Mot (Death) is described as entering through windows. The specific targeting of children in the streets and young men in the squares (ʾăpiqîm, also translated as playgrounds/public squares) describes the urban massacre: the most visible and unprotected members of the community are killed first. The image appears in Lamentations 2:21 as fulfilled historical narrative.
22: 'Human corpses will fall like manure on the open field, like grain stalks left behind by the reaper, with no one to gather them up' — the two comparison images are both agricultural: manure (the waste product of agriculture, abundant but valueless) and abandoned grain stalks (the reaper's leftovers). Both images describe bodies treated as refuse rather than as persons deserving burial rites. The 'no one to gather them up' (wĕʾên mĕʾassēp) is the denial of the harvesting function — the bodies lie in the field unrecovered, as if the community itself has ceased to function as a community.
23: 'Let not the wise boast in their wisdom, nor the mighty in their strength, nor the rich in their wealth' — the three boasting prohibitions cover the standard ANE triads of human achievement: wisdom (the scribal class), strength (the warrior class), and wealth (the merchant class). Each was a form of social power that provided security in the human sphere. In the context of Jeremiah's time, these were also the three grounds on which the Jerusalem establishment justified its confidence in survival: they had wise advisors, a military, and economic resources. The oracle strips each ground of confidence by subordinating it to covenant knowledge.
24: 'But let him who boasts boast in this: that he understands and knows me — that I am the LORD who exercises steadfast love, justice, and righteousness in the earth' — the three divine character qualities (ḥesed, mišpāṭ, ṣĕdāqāh) that YHWH declares he delights in are the exact covenant qualities that Israel's prophets consistently accuse the people of lacking. Ḥesed (steadfast love/covenant loyalty), mišpāṭ (justice), and ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) are the Hosea 6:6, Amos 5:24, and Micah 6:8 triad. Paul quotes the verse's first half in 1 Corinthians 1:31 and 2 Corinthians 10:17, applying it to the cross as the proper ground of Christian boasting.
25: 'The days are coming, declares the LORD, when I will punish all who are only outwardly circumcised' — the 'circumcised in foreskin' (kol-mûl bĕʿorlāh) but uncircumcised in heart extends the judgment beyond Israel to the surrounding nations. The passage (Jeremiah 9:25-26) is unusual: it places Judah in the same category as Egypt, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and the desert peoples who practice circumcision as a custom but not as a covenant sign. Paul's argument in Romans 2:25-29 and Galatians 5:6 builds directly on this prophetic tradition: physical circumcision without covenant faithfulness is equivalent to non-circumcision.
26: 'Egypt, Judah, Edom, Ammon, Moab, and all who live in the wilderness and clip the hair at their temples — for all these nations are physically uncircumcised, and all the house of Israel is uncircumcised in heart' — the nations listed (except possibly the desert peoples) practiced various forms of circumcision. Egypt circumcised as a coming-of-age rite; several Semitic peoples practiced it as a pre-marriage rite. The point is that circumcision as a physical practice was not distinctively Israelite — what was distinctive was its function as the covenant sign. Israel has reduced the covenant sign to the level of a pagan custom by maintaining the physical rite while ignoring the covenant commitment it represents. Deuteronomy 10:16 and 30:6 issued the same corrective.
